snap_subtitle: The G305 killer with flagship specs and hot-swap switches.
keytakeaway: The Asus ROG Strix Impact III Wireless is a lightweight, dual-mode gaming mouse that outperforms its budget rivals by using the flagship AimPoint sensor and hot-swappable switches. Powered by AA/AAA batteries, it offers a "forever mouse" appeal for FPS and MOBA players who want top-tier tracking without the built-in battery decay.
pcard: The ROG Strix Impact III Wireless is a **battery-powered wireless gaming mouse** that brings flagship performance to the mid-range market. With its 36k DPI AimPoint sensor and ~57 g (bare) shell, it directly challenges the <pop=mouse-568>Logitech G305</pop> by offering better tracking, Bluetooth support, and hot-swappable switches. It’s a fantastic choice for claw and fingertip players who want a durable, repairable mouse for competitive shooters.
verdict:
The **Asus ROG Strix Impact III Wireless** is a battery-powered **wireless gaming mouse** designed for claw and fingertip players who want modern tracking in Valorant, CS2, Apex Legends, and other aim-heavy games—without committing to an internal rechargeable pack. It pairs the **ROG AimPoint sensor** (36,000 DPI, <1% deviation, 650 IPS, 50G) with **SpeedNova 2.4GHz** and Bluetooth, so it can be both a competitive desk mouse and a genuinely practical travel mouse. The standout feature is the **Push-Fit Switch Socket** design: when the main clicks wear or develop issues, you can swap compatible mechanical switches instead of replacing the entire mouse.

#### Lineup context
Positioned below the <pop=mouse-53>ROG Harpe Ace Aim Lab Edition</pop>, this model acts as the “value + longevity” option in Asus’s lineup. You give up an integrated battery and the absolute lightest weight, but you gain long-term ownership perks: **replaceable batteries**, **repairable main clicks**, and a compact, familiar shape.

#### Shape, grip, and balance
The shell sticks to a compact, symmetrical “egg” profile with **flatter sidewalls** than the <pop=mouse-102>Logitech G305 Lightspeed</pop>. In practice, that feels less cramped and makes it easier to reposition your fingers for small vertical aim corrections—useful for recoil control and steady tracking in CS2/Valorant. It’s a safer fit for **claw and fingertip** than full palm—especially if your hands are larger and you like a taller rear hump.

Asus advertises a 57 g shell, but the mouse can’t run without a battery—and where that weight sits affects how “snappy” it feels during starts/stops. The good news: you get a **AAA-to-AA adapter** in the box, so you can tune the feel instead of being locked into a heavy, rear-biased setup.

| Battery setup | Feel & balance in play |
|---|---|
| **AA (convenience)** | Heavier, more back-weighted; fine for mixed work/gaming, but less controlled on fast stops |
| **AA lithium** | Better than alkaline, still somewhat rear-biased |
| **AAA lithium + adapter (recommended)** | More neutral balance and easier lifts/resets for low-sens FPS play |

> If you like replaceable-battery mice but hate outdated sensors and “throw it away when it double-clicks” ownership, this one makes a strong case.

#### Performance, wireless, and setup
In 2.4GHz mode, **1,000 Hz polling** is the sweet spot for competitive FPS, and Asus rates battery life up to **450 hours** with lighting off (on an AA lithium). Bluetooth stretches endurance further (up to **618 hours** with lighting off) and can pair up to three devices; it’s great for travel and productivity, but treat it as a convenience mode rather than your ranked-queue link. Onboard controls plus **onboard memory** let you adjust core settings directly on the mouse, then optionally use Armoury Crate for deeper remaps or lighting before uninstalling after you’ve saved a profile.

#### Clicks, materials, and reliability notes
The hot-swap sockets dramatically reduce long-term risk from switch wear. Primary buttons use durable **PBT** tops that resist shine and keep their texture over time. The side shell uses a smooth matte plastic rather than a tacky rubber coat, so dry hands may want grip tape for effortless lifts/resets. Layout is simple—two side buttons—perfect for FPS and most MOBA binds, but not an MMO grid.

#### How it compares
Think of this as the “<pop=mouse-102>G305</pop> idea” modernized: similar size and control, but with Bluetooth and user-serviceable clicks. It’s steadier than an <pop=mouse-140>Orochi V2</pop> for relaxed claw, and more sustainable than a <pop=mouse-53>Harpe Ace</pop> thanks to replaceable batteries. If you value longevity over chasing the lightest possible shell, this lands in the right spot.

#### Bottom line
Run it on the **AAA adapter setup**, save your profile to onboard memory, and you get a **fast, flexible, repair-friendly mouse** that’s one of the easiest recommendations in the battery-powered segment. It’s strong in tactical shooters, travel-friendly, and built to outlast most sealed-battery rivals. The coating and Armoury Crate bloat are the main nitpicks—but they’re easy to live with considering the performance and lifespan advantages.

For a curated short list tailored to tactical shooters, see our [best wireless gaming mice for Valorant](/guides/mouse/top-10-wireless) guide.
pros: Flagship ROG AimPoint sensor (36k DPI, 650 IPS) provides esports-grade tracking
Push-Fit Switch Sockets enable instant repairs and customizable click feel
Dual-mode connectivity (2.4GHz + Bluetooth) offers flexible use
PBT buttons resist shine and surface wear over time
Excellent battery life (450h on 2.4GHz / 618h on Bluetooth)
Lightweight potential (~68 g) with AAA lithium configuration
cons: Weight balance is rear-heavy with standard AA batteries
Coating can feel slippery for users with dry hands
Armoury Crate software is bloated and resource-heavy
Scroll wheel lacks the same premium feel as main buttons
